title: Replacing a toilet flush valve
subtitle: Turn off the supply, empty the cistern, swap the valve mechanism
excerpt: How to replace a worn or failing toilet flush valve (siphon or dual-flush) in a close-coupled or wall-mounted cistern: isolating the supply, emptying the cistern, unbolting the valve, and fitting the replacement.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Fluidmaster and Siamp technical installation guides (publicly available). British Standard BS 1212.

content.text: A toilet that runs continuously, trickles into the bowl between flushes, or requires multiple handle presses to flush usually has a failing
flush valve

. Modern toilets use either a siphon (traditional UK type) or a dual-flush tower valve. Both are replaced the same way: turn off the supply, empty the cistern, unbolt the valve, and fit the replacement. The entire job usually takes under 90 minutes.
Step 1, isolate the supply, drain the section, catch the residual

Close the isolating valve on the supply pipe to the cistern (behind the toilet or below the cistern). Flush the toilet to empty the cistern. Sponge out or mop up any residual water from the cistern base; there will be a shallow pool that does not drain by flushing. Have a bowl and towel ready.
Removing the old flush valve
Step 2, disconnect the overflow pipe.
If the cistern has a separate overflow pipe, detach it from the flush valve or cistern outlet.
Step 3, remove the flush valve.

For a siphon flush valve: the siphon is held in the cistern by a large back-nut on the underside of the cistern, accessed from below. On a close-coupled cistern, remove the cistern-to-pan bolts and lift the cistern clear to access the base. Unscrew the siphon back-nut with the water pump pliers. Lift the siphon out through the top.

For a dual-flush tower valve: the tower clips or screws into the cistern outlet. Turn the tower anticlockwise (as viewed from above) by a quarter-turn to release it from its bayonet fitting. Lift it straight out.
Fitting the new flush valve
Step 4, set the overflow height.

For siphon types: check the overflow height of the new siphon matches the overflow outlet height in the cistern. For dual-flush types: adjust the tower height to sit 25 mm below the cistern water-level mark before fitting.
Step 5, fit and secure.

Lower the new valve into the cistern outlet. For a siphon: fit the new back-nut from below and tighten until firm. For a dual-flush tower: align the bayonet lugs and turn clockwise a quarter-turn to lock. Connect the flush handle or push-button mechanism to the new valve per the fitting instructions.
Step 6, also check the
ballcock

While the cistern is empty, check that the fill valve washer is in good condition. A fill valve that drips after refilling indicates a worn seat; replace the fill valve at the same time to avoid a repeat job.
Step 7, restore and test.

Refit the cistern if removed. Slowly open the isolation valve and allow the cistern to refill. Watch the water level reach the fill line and stop. Flush several times and check for drips at the base-nut.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Adjust the float arm: on a traditional float, bend the arm slightly downward to lower the shut-off level. On a piston fill valve, turn the adjustment screw on the valve body. Replace the washer if the valve drips after adjustment.
attrs.cause: Fill valve (ballcock) set too high, or the float or valve washer is worn.

attrs.fix: Lower the tower to reduce the water level in the cistern. Check the rubber seal is clean and seated flat.
attrs.cause: Dual-flush tower seal not seating correctly, or overflow height set too high.
attrs.symptom: Toilet runs continuously after new valve fitted
